figures.caption: Fig. 15–1.A rectangular loop carrying the current II sits in a uniform field B\FigB (in the zz-direction). The torque on the loop is τ=μ×B\Figtau=\Figmu\times\FigB, where the magnetic moment μ=Iab\mu=Iab.
Fig. 15–2.A loop is carried along the xx-direction through the field B\FigB, at right angles to xx.
Fig. 15–3.Finding the energy of a small loop in a magnetic field.
Figure 15-4.The energy of a large loop in a magnetic field can be considered as the sum of energies of smaller loops.
Figure 15-5.An interference experiment with electrons (see also Chapter 37 of Vol. I).
Figure 15-6.The magnetic field and vector potential of a long solenoid.
Figure 15-7.A magnetic field can influence the motion of electrons even though it exists only in regions where there is an arbitrarily small probability of finding the electrons.
Figure 15-8.The shift of the interference pattern due to a strip of magnetic field.
Figure 15-9.Deflection of a particle due to passage through a strip of magnetic field.

Figure 15-10.The potentials at point [math](1) and at the time [math]t are given by summing the contributions from each element of the source at the roving point [math](2), using the currents and charges which were present at the earlier time [math]t-r_{12}/c.
